basic_info.name: Thermoset Curing Agent Selection
basic_info.name_ja: 熱硬化性樹脂用硬化剤選定
basic_info.description: Hansen Solubility Parameters are used to predict the miscibility and mixing homogeneity between thermoset resins (epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester) and curing agents (amines, anhydrides, phenolics). The HSP distance between resin and hardener governs initial mixing quality, which in turn affects pot life, gelation uniformity, and ultimately the crosslink density and mechanical properties of the cured network. A low RED value indicates thermodynamic compatibility, promoting fine-scale mixing before cure onset, while excessive HSP mismatch can cause phase separation during cure, leading to heterogeneous crosslink networks and degraded performance.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: The core principle is that resin-hardener compatibility is governed by the HSP distance (Ra) between the two components. When Ra is small relative to the interaction radius R0 of the resin, the hardener is thermodynamically miscible and will form a homogeneous mixture. During cure, the system undergoes gelation and vitrification; if the initial mixture is well-mixed at the molecular level (low RED), the resulting crosslinked network will be uniform. High Ra leads to micro-phase separation before or during cure, creating domains of under-cured and over-cured material. The Flory-Huggins interaction parameter chi, derived from Ra, can be used to estimate the free energy of mixing and predict the phase behavior of the uncured blend.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra = sqrt(4*(dD1-dD2)^2 + (dP1-dP2)^2 + (dH1-dH2)^2); RED = Ra/R0; chi = V_hardener * Ra^2 / (6*R*T)
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED < 1.0 for good miscibility; RED < 0.7 preferred for optimal mixing homogeneity before gelation

required_inputs: HSP of curing agent (dD, dP, dH)
data_sources: HSPiP database (extensive epoxy and amine data)
Group contribution methods (Van Krevelen, Hoftyzer) for novel hardeners
Published literature values for common resin-hardener systems
Inverse gas chromatography for specialty resins
complementary_parameters: Equivalent weight of resin and hardener
Viscosity of components at mixing temperature
Gel time and pot life data
Glass transition temperature (Tg) of cured system

limitations: HSP predicts thermodynamic miscibility of uncured components but does not capture cure kinetics or reaction-induced phase separation
Highly reactive systems may gel before achieving equilibrium mixing, making initial HSP matching less predictive
Crosslinking changes the effective HSP of the network during cure, so initial compatibility may not reflect final morphology
Multi-component formulations (accelerators, fillers, tougheners) complicate simple binary HSP matching
Anhydride hardeners with catalysts follow different mixing dynamics than amine systems
Does not account for specific chemical interactions (e.g., Lewis acid-base catalysis) that affect cure behavior
known_failure_modes: Reaction-induced phase separation (RIPS): initially compatible blends can phase-separate during cure as molecular weight increases
Latent hardeners designed for poor initial miscibility but cure at elevated temperature
Systems with strong hydrogen bonding between hardener and resin that are not captured by averaged dH values
Catalytic curing agents used at very low stoichiometric ratios where bulk miscibility is less relevant
validation_method: Optical microscopy or SEM of cured cross-sections for phase homogeneity
DSC measurement of Tg breadth (narrow Tg = homogeneous network)
DMA tan-delta peak width as homogeneity indicator
Gel time measurement at controlled temperature
Mechanical testing (flexural strength, fracture toughness) of cured specimens

key_references.author: Hansen, C.M.
key_references.title: Hansen Solubility Parameters: A User's Handbook, 2nd Edition

key_references.author: Mezzenga, R.; Boogh, L.; Manson, J.-A.E.
key_references.title: A review of dendritic hyperbranched polymer as modifiers in epoxy composites

key_references.author: Prolongo, S.G.; Cabanelas, J.C.; Baselga, J.
key_references.title: Reactive compatibilization of epoxy/polyorganosiloxane blends

key_references.author: Pascault, J.-P.; Williams, R.J.J.
key_references.title: Epoxy Polymers: New Materials and Innovations

literature.regulatory_relevance: Not directly referenced in regulatory frameworks, but HSP-based formulation design supports REACH compliance by enabling substitution of hazardous solvents/diluents in thermoset formulations. Some aerospace specifications indirectly rely on compatibility screening that aligns with HSP principles.
